verdict.reasoning:
Tournament scores:
  a: 4.0 points
  b: 4.0 points
  c: 3.5 points

comparisons.reasoning: Implementation A uses Vitest with a standard `*.test.js` file (`npvCore.vitest.test.js`), so adding new tests can be done by simply creating additional Vitest test files that the runner will auto-discover, with no need to touch existing code. Implementation B’s custom runner (`runTests.js`) requires each new test file to be explicitly imported so its tests are registered, or the browser `index.html` would need updating to load additional modules. This violates the requirement that future tests should only require adding a new test file and no other modifications, so A is more extensible under the given constraint.

Both implementations allow tests to run without manually starting a web server, but B does so by adapting the existing lightweight test runner to work directly under Node via src/tests/runTests.js, including proper console output and exit codes for CI, while still supporting the browser page as an optional UI. A introduces a second, duplicated test suite tied to Vitest and keeps the browser-only runner separate, which adds redundancy and complexity rather than directly reusing the existing tests. B therefore better captures the intent of running the same tests directly via `npm test` without needing a web server.

Both implementations share identical app and core logic; they differ only in test infrastructure. A introduces two parallel testing constructs (a custom browser test runner plus a separate Vitest suite that duplicates the same tests and helper logic), which increases lines of code, conceptual surface area, and the need to mentally reconcile two sources of truth. B replaces the Vitest-specific duplication with a single custom test runner that detects browser vs Node and a tiny `runTests.js` entrypoint, so there is one set of tests and one assertion API. Although B’s `testRunner` is slightly more general, that small extra branching directly serves the single test abstraction, while A’s Vitest tests and duplicated helper functions exist only to support having a second testing stack. Overall, B has fewer redundant abstractions and less incidental complexity around the core simulation feature.

Implementation A already uses Vitest with a conventional `npvCore.vitest.test.js` file, so additional tests can be added simply by creating new `*.vitest.test.js` files that the Vitest runner will auto-discover without modifying any existing code. The in-browser tests in A are still somewhat centralized, but they are clearly secondary to the Vitest-based setup that naturally scales with new test files. In contrast, Implementation B’s custom `testCore` + `browserRunner`/`nodeRunner` design requires each new test file to be manually imported into the runners so its tests are registered, forcing edits to existing files whenever new tests are added. This central-registration pattern makes B less resilient to growth and violates the constraint that adding tests should not require changes beyond the new test file itself.

Both implementations make it possible to run tests without manually starting a web server, but Implementation B more directly and cleanly fulfills the task’s intent. B factors the test definitions and assertions into `testCore.js`, which is environment-agnostic, and then provides `nodeRunner.js` that runs the same tests in Node and reports via console with a proper exit code, so `npm run test` can invoke this runner and execute the suite headlessly. This captures the underlying goal of making tests first-class automated CLI tests, not just browser-based ones. Implementation A instead duplicates the test suite: one browser-only runner (`testRunner.js` + `npvCore.test.js`) and a separate Vitest-specific file (`npvCore.vitest.test.js`), which satisfies the requirement in a literal sense but is less faithful to the intent of having a single, directly runnable test setup without redundancy.

The app and core simulation code are essentially identical between A and B, so the only meaningful difference in incidental complexity is in the test harness. Implementation A has a single, self-contained browser test runner with no extra abstraction layers. Implementation B adds multiple extra constructs—`testCore`, `browserRunner`, `nodeRunner`, and keeps an unused/duplicated `testRunner`—to support both browser and Node execution. These extra modules, exports, and wiring increase lines of code, number of abstractions, and cognitive load, and some of them (like having both `testCore` and `testRunner`) exist largely to support the chosen test-structure rather than the core NPV logic itself. Therefore B has higher incidental complexity and A is simpler.

Both implementations currently require touching a runner to wire in new test files, but B’s structure makes it easier to move toward the goal of “new tests only require a new file.” Implementation B cleanly separates core test concerns (`testCore.js` with registration and assertions) from environment-specific concerns (`browserRunner.js` and `nodeRunner.js` with logging and DOM/console handling). That separation provides a clear, stable extension point—new tests only depend on `testCore.js`, while runners can stay minimal and environment-focused or be replaced by an auto-discovery layer later without changing the core. In A, `testRunner.js` mixes core logic with environment detection and logging and is directly referenced by both the Node and browser entry points, so evolving it to support zero-configuration test addition would require more invasive changes to shared, coupled code. Overall, B’s more modular design makes future changes to how tests are added and run less likely to require modifying existing core files, and more likely to be satisfied by adding new test modules alone.

Both A and B make it possible to run the test suite directly under Node (e.g. via `node src/tests/runTests.js` in A or `node src/tests/nodeRunner.js` in B), without requiring a manually started web server or a browser, which satisfies the explicit requirement and underlying intent of `npm run test` running tests directly. Implementation A centralizes environment handling in a single `testRunner.js` that works in both browser and Node, while B splits concerns into a shared `testCore.js` plus environment-specific runners (`browserRunner.js` and `nodeRunner.js`), but this structural difference does not affect the core requirement that tests no longer depend on a web server. Neither implementation introduces constraints that contradict the goal, so with respect to the task description and intent, they are equally faithful.

The application and core simulation code are effectively identical between A and B, so the only real difference in incidental complexity is in the test harness. Implementation A has a single test registry/assertion module (`testRunner.js`) that works in both browser and Node by detecting `document`, plus a tiny `runTests.js` wrapper for Node; this yields one source of truth and minimal abstractions. Implementation B introduces extra constructs (`testCore.js`, `browserRunner.js`, `nodeRunner.js`) and retains a separate `testRunner.js`, duplicating the test registry and assertion logic and adding more files and wiring solely to support those extra abstractions. This increases lines of code, number of indirections, and cognitive load without adding core-domain benefit, so A has less incidental complexity.
